clauses.descriptipn:
Option A (Arbitration): Any disputes arising from the contract, including non-contractual obligations, are finally resolved through SIAC arbitration in Singapore, with one arbitrator, English language, and governed by Singapore law.
Option B (Court Jurisdiction): The parties submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Singapore courts.
New York Clause: Parties submit to the non‑exclusive jurisdiction of New York state or federal courts (Manhattan), waive venue or forum non conveniens objections, and may bring proceedings in multiple jurisdictions. Additionally, there is an irrevocable waiver of jury trial for any proceedings.

clauses.description:
Ownership: Each party keeps its own Background IP; no transfer.
Licence to Customer: Supplier grants a perpetual, royalty‑free, irrevocable, worldwide licence to use, modify, and create derivatives of Supplier’s Background IP only to receive the supplies (not for commercialisation). Customer may sublicense to service providers or group members for the same purpose.
Third Party IP: Supplier must licence it on similar terms; if not possible, propose terms or find an alternative. No third party IP without Customer’s consent.
Licence to Supplier: Customer grants a revocable, non‑exclusive licence to use Customer’s Background IP solely to perform Supplier’s obligations.
Warranties: Supplier warrants no infringement, no extra fees for Customer. Customer warrants similar for its IP.
Infringement notice: Supplier must promptly notify Customer of any alleged IP infringement.

clauses.description:
Customer consent required: Supplier cannot subcontract any obligations without the Customer's prior written consent.
Notification & details: Supplier must notify Customer of intended subcontractors (and any changes), provide requested details, and obtain consent for changes. Customer may object.
Supplier remains liable: Supplier is fully liable for subcontractor’s performance as if it were its own acts/omissions.
Subcontract terms: Must be written, consistent with the contract, and capable of novation to Customer at no extra cost.
Breach consequences: Engaging a subcontractor without consent is a material breach, entitling Customer to terminate.
Customer can require replacement: Customer may demand Supplier terminate a subcontractor if the subcontractor materially breaches, performs unsatisfactorily, damages Customer’s reputation, or was engaged improperly. Supplier must then use an alternative or perform itself.

clauses.description: 29.  ASSIGNMENT   29.1  Subject to clauses 29.2 and 29.3, neither Party may assign, transfer, novate or otherwise deal with its rights or obligations under the Contract Document without the other Party's prior written consent (which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld).  29.2  The Customer may assign, transfer, novate or otherwise deal with the Customer's rights or obligations under the Contract Document in whole or in part at any time to any Customer Group member or any successor of all or part of the Customer's business without the Supplier's consent.   29.3  If required by the Customer, the Supplier shall enter into documentation in a form provided by the Customer to give effect to the assignment, transfer or novation of, or other dealing with, the Contract Document in accordance with clause 29.2.

clauses.description: 35.5 Third party rights  35.5.1 The Contract Document does not create or confer any rights or benefits enforceable by any person not a party to it except that to the extent any rights or benefits are conferred to members of the Customer Group:  (A) each member of the Customer Group shall have the right to enforce any rights or benefits conferred on it in the Contract Document against the Supplier directly; and  (B) each member of the Customer Group shall have the right to enforce the rights or benefits of any indemnity, limitation or exclusion of liability in the Contract Document against the Supplier directly.  35.5.2 No consent from the persons referred to in this clause is required for the Parties to vary or rescind the Contract Document (whether or not in a way that varies or extinguishes rights or benefits in favour of such Third Parties).

clauses.description:
Unlimited liabilities (22.1): death/injury, property damage, indemnities, deliberate repudiatory breach, dishonesty/fraud, wilful misconduct, and anything not limitable by law. Charge deductions (liquidated damages, service credits) are excluded from cap calculations.
No indirect/consequential damages (22.2), subject to exceptions.
Liability cap (22.3): per event – for physical property damage and all other liability – capped at a specified amount or 300% of total charges (whichever greater). Charges are defined broadly (22.4) as if the contract was fully performed.
Customer recoverable losses (22.5): a long, non‑exhaustive list including cost of alternative software, data restoration, workarounds, regulatory penalties, third‑party claims, remediation costs, and even consequential loss from Supplier’s negligence – plus breaches of confidentiality, publicity, conflict of interest, or regulatory obligations.
Customer can recover for its group members (22.6) directly.

clauses.description:
General duty: Recipient must not disclose or use Disclosing Party’s confidential information without prior consent.
Exceptions (info not protected): public domain (not due to breach), already known from another source, or independently developed.
Permitted disclosures: to personnel/advisers, Customer group members, auditors, and Customer service providers – provided they sign binding confidentiality undertakings (no less onerous terms).
Required disclosure by law: recipient must give notice and cooperate to limit disclosure.
Return/destruction: On Customer’s request (or when no longer needed), Supplier must delete/destroy Customer confidential information using auditable methods (e.g., NIST or DoD standards) and certify in writing.
Equitable relief: Injunctions and specific performance available because damages are inadequate.
Third party IP: Supplier may need to sign separate undertakings with Customer’s third party providers.

clauses.description:
Tiered requirements: Depending on risk (low‑risk cloud/on‑premise, material outsourcing, etc.), different versions apply – from basic “appropriate security measures” to a full long‑form with detailed controls.
Core obligation: Supplier must protect Customer Confidential Information using Good Industry Practice – confidentiality, integrity, availability, resilience.
Long‑form adds:

Compliance with ISO 27001, NIST, PCI DSS (if card data).
Third Party Security Assessment (TPSA) on Customer’s request (up to yearly, more after incidents).
Remedial action plan for failures (90 days), with termination for non‑remediation.
Incident reporting (Material Cyber Security Incident):

1 hour for “Relevant Incident” (severe impact); 24 hours for others (2 hours in India for material outsourcing).
Root cause analysis (RCA) within 10 days, final within 28 days.
Detailed security controls in an appendix (minimum 13 sections): human resource security, risk management, asset inventory, access control (MFA for privileged accounts), encryption (AES‑128, TLS 1.2), network security, physical security, backup, continuous monitoring, annual penetration testing, etc.
Survival: The annexure survives termination.

clauses.description:
Roles: Customer = data controller, Supplier = data processor.
Core obligations: Supplier must comply with all Data Protection Laws (GDPR, UK GDPR, etc.), only process Personal Data on Customer’s documented instructions, implement appropriate technical/organisational measures, assist with data subject rights and data breach responses.
Subprocessing: Prior written authorisation required (specific or general). Supplier remains fully liable for subprocessors.
Inspections/audits: Supplier must allow Customer (or its auditor) to inspect compliance at no extra cost.
Data breaches: Notify Customer within 24 hours (or earlier if required by regulator).
International transfers: If Personal Data goes to a Third Country, Supplier must comply with EU Standard Contractual Clauses (Module 2) and UK Addendum (as applicable). The annexure includes full SCCs and UK Addendum as schedules.
Deletion/return: On contract end, Supplier must delete or return all Personal Data.
Survival: Annexure survives termination.

clauses.description:
Independent controllers: Each party acts as a separate controller for Shared Personal Data (unless they later agree joint controller terms).
Core obligations: Each complies with Data Protection Laws, processes only for the Agreed Purpose, implements appropriate security, assists with data subject requests and breach notifications.
Shared Personal Data: Defined in Schedule 1 (types, categories, purpose, duration).
Subcontracting: Supplier may share with sub‑processors (written contract with equivalent obligations) or other controllers (written agreement), remains fully liable.
Cross‑border transfers: No transfer to a Third Country without Customer’s prior written consent. Any Restricted Transfer must use EU Standard Contractual Clauses (Module 1 – Controller to Controller) and UK Addendum (set out in Schedules 2 & 3). Supplier must notify Customer of recipient, location, and transfer mechanism.
Risk assessments: Supplier must conduct data transfer impact assessments at its own cost if Customer reasonably requires.
Survival: The annexure survives termination.

clauses.description: This Data Privacy Annexure establishes a joint controller framework under EU and UK GDPR, requiring both parties to process personal data only for a specified permitted purpose, maintain lawful bases, implement security controls (per an Information Security Annexure), and cooperate on data breaches, subject access requests, and complaints—with the Customer holding ultimate decision-making authority if the parties cannot agree. The Supplier cannot engage sub-processors or transfer data to a third country without the Customer’s prior written consent, and any restricted transfer must use the EU Standard Contractual Clauses (Module 1, Controller-to-Controller) and the UK Addendum, including transfer impact assessments at the Supplier’s cost. Detailed processing descriptions are set out in Schedule 1, the EU SCCs in Schedule 2, and the UK Addendum in Schedule 3, and the entire clause survives termination of the main agreement.

clauses.description: This Responsible Artificial Intelligence clause requires the Supplier to fully disclose any AI used in connection with the services, ensure it complies with the eight enumerated RAI Principles (covering data suitability, explainability, fairness/bias/ethics, stability/overfitting, assessment validation, ongoing monitoring, auditability, and third-party oversight), and maintain sufficient logs and evidence to explain all AI-driven decisions. The Supplier must provide 120 days’ notice before making material changes to AI models and, upon the Customer’s written request, furnish detailed information regarding data inputs, training data selection, bias and validation assessments, ongoing monitoring, and any privacy or cyber security findings. Additionally, the Supplier must assist the Customer in meeting transparency obligations under applicable law, including customer-facing statements or regulatory disclosures about AI use.

clauses.description: This anti-bribery and corruption clause requires the Supplier to represent, warrant, and undertake that it, its employees, and its agents will not make or offer any improper payment, gift, or advantage that violates applicable laws (including the UK Bribery Act 2010 and US FCPA), influences a Public Official, or is otherwise unethical or illegal. The Supplier must remain compliant with all relevant anti-bribery laws, not cause the Customer to breach such laws, and immediately disclose any non-compliance to the Customer. These obligations extend to any subcontractors or third parties engaged by the Supplier, for whom the Supplier remains fully liable. A breach or the Customer’s reasonable belief of a breach allows the Customer to terminate the contract without compensation to the Supplier. The clause also defines key terms, including Public Official (covering government employees, state-owned entity staff, royal family members, political figures, Politically Exposed Persons, and others), State Owned Entity (based on ownership or control thresholds), and Politically Exposed Persons.

clauses.description: This clause contains two anti-bribery options. The standard version (Clause 2) prohibits improper payments, requires compliance with laws like the UK Bribery Act and US FCPA, extends obligations to subcontractors, and allows the Customer to terminate without compensation upon any breach or suspected breach. The long-form version (Clause 3) is mandatory for high-risk countries (Iraq, Bangladesh, Cambodia, Myanmar, Nigeria) or high-risk intermediaries. It adds requirements for Supplier Group Companies, anti-corruption training, audit rights, record-keeping, immediate breach notification, and defines
and

clauses.description: The Supplier must maintain a Business Continuity Plan and Crisis Management Plan, reviewed and tested annually, covering scenarios like building loss, staff unavailability, tech failure, and fourth-party disruptions. Plans require Customer approval, with modifications as reasonably requested. The Supplier must notify the Customer immediately of any operational disruption or crisis, implement the plans when needed, and share test results. If disruption or crisis prevents performance for more than ten business days, the Customer may terminate. For services supporting the Customer’s Important Business Services (IBS), the Supplier must align with impact tolerance and participate in scenario testing, with termination if unable to comply.

clauses.description: Under this clause, the Supplier acknowledges that the Customer or its group members may need to restructure, wind down, or divest as part of a recovery or resolution plan (e.g., under UK Banking Act 2009 or equivalent laws). If a resolution occurs or a group member’s financial position deteriorates, the Supplier must continue providing services to any affected entity or restructured entity at no extra charge, as if no restructuring happened. The Supplier must consent to assignment or novation of the contract to the restructured entity, enter into a separate contract on substantially similar terms if requested, facilitate an uninterrupted transfer of services, and cannot terminate, vary, or suspend services due to the resolution or financial deterioration. These obligations last as long as the Customer reasonably requires.

clauses.clause_key: Release Management and Change Management Services@Software Maintenance Schedule
clauses.description: The Supplier must keep the Customer informed of all new releases (modifications, enhancements, new versions) of the maintained software, offer them immediately when available, and upon Customer request, deliver, install, integrate, test, and assist with testing—repeating until acceptance criteria are met. If a new release fails acceptance, the Supplier must help decommission it and restore the prior state. Accepted new releases become part of the maintained software at no additional charge, and training is provided free if requested. The Customer is not obliged to accept any new release, but the Supplier may not be liable for defects that could have been fixed by an accepted release the Customer declined. The Supplier must support the current version and at least the two prior versions for a minimum of two years. All releases require written Customer approval and must be deployed with minimal business disruption, including detailed release plans, notifications, demonstrations, training, and risk assessments for patches and critical vulnerabilities.

clauses.clause_key: Service Levels/Key Performance Indicators (KPI)@ Cloud Services Schedule
clauses.description: The Supplier must meet or exceed the Availability and Service Level standards specified in the Order Form, excluding downtime caused by the Customer, internet issues, scheduled maintenance (with 72 hours’ notice, max one hour, during maintenance windows), or Customer system failures. Monthly Service Level Reports must detail performance, failures, remedies, and credits due. Service Credits are a price adjustment, not the sole remedy, and the Customer may apply them as a credit or cash payment. Availability failures trigger termination rights: if Availability drops below required levels for two consecutive months, any three non-consecutive months in a year, or below 95% in any month, the Customer may terminate immediately without early termination charges. Additionally, if Availability fails for three consecutive months or any three months in a six-month period, the Customer may terminate with no further liability. The Supplier must use approved monitoring tools, immediately notify the Customer of any delays, degradations, or failures with root cause analysis, and cannot rely on force majeure excuses for Availability failures. The Customer may also terminate if any material change impacts the Supplier’s ability to perform.

clauses.clause_key: Service Levels/Key Performance Indicators (KPI)@ IT Outsourced Managed Services Schedule
clauses.description: The Supplier must conduct monthly performance review meetings with the Customer, covering Service Level attainment, planned and emergency outages, and service improvements. For Managed Services, the Supplier must meet specified Service Levels or, if none are specified, Good Industry Practice. The Supplier is a single point of contact for all failures, even those caused by subcontractors. Upon any Service Level failure, the Supplier must pay Service Credits (a price adjustment, not the sole remedy) and remedy, minimize impact, and prevent recurrence. The Customer may apply credits as cash or invoice deduction. The Supplier must use Customer-approved monitoring tools, promptly notify the Customer of any actual or anticipated failures, delays, or degradations, provide root cause analysis and recommendations, and take necessary action (including additional resources) to resolve issues. The Supplier must also implement monitoring tools to track performance and provide reports as required.

clauses.clause_key: Service Levels/Key Performance Indicators (KPI)@ Data Centre Services Schedule
clauses.description: The Supplier must meet specified Service Levels for Data Centre Services, excluding downtime caused by Customer misuse or external network issues. Scheduled downtime requires 72 hours’ notice, lasts ≤1 hour, occurs during maintenance windows, and should avoid regular business hours. If Service Levels are not met, the Supplier must pay Service Credits (a price adjustment, not the sole remedy), remedy failures, minimize impact, and prevent recurrence. Force majeure does not excuse Availability failures (excluding telecom, power, due diligence failures, or internal issues). The Customer may apply Service Credits as cash or credit. The Supplier must use Customer-approved monitoring tools, immediately notify the Customer of any actual or anticipated failures, delays, or degradations, provide root cause analysis, and take necessary action. If Availability fails for three consecutive months or any three months in a six-month period, the Customer may terminate with no further liability.

Service Credits are in addition to and do not prejudice any other remedies available to the Customer.

clauses.description: The Supplier must ensure Services meet or exceed Service Levels throughout the term. If Service Levels are not met, the Supplier must pay Service Credits (a debt due, deductible from invoices or paid directly) and remedy, minimize impact, and prevent recurrence. Service Credits are not the sole remedy, survive termination, and represent a refund for reduced value—they do not preclude the Customer from claiming additional damages (reduced by the credit amount). The Supplier must minimize Service Failures, notify the Customer immediately of actual or anticipated failures, provide root cause analysis and corrective procedures, and take necessary action (including additional resources). The Supplier must also implement Customer-approved monitoring tools to measure and report performance against Service Levels.

clauses.description: The Supplier cannot subcontract without the Customer’s prior written consent (at least 90 days’ notice required). The Supplier remains fully liable for all subcontracted work, must ensure subcontractors comply with contract terms, and enable novation to the Customer at no cost. The Customer may object to any subcontractor change; unauthorized subcontracting is a material breach allowing termination. The Customer can also require removal of a subcontractor for poor performance, reputational harm, or regulatory concerns—following a mandatory transition plan. Data transfer to subcontractors needs specific written authorization. Upon termination for cause, the Customer may require novation of key subcontracts.

clauses.description: The Supplier may only perform obligations from approved Service Locations (Customer sites, agreed Order Form locations, or dedicated facilities). Any change requires a written Change agreement and an approved migration plan (with a structured review process; if not approved, locations remain unchanged). Before relocating, the Supplier must address data protection, security, and demonstrate no impact on obligations, rights, or charges—migration costs are borne by the Supplier unless the Customer requested the change. For dedicated facilities, the Customer and regulators have unfettered access, and access is restricted to authorized personnel. Cost savings from a dedicated facility relocation must be shared with the Customer. Dedicated facilities cannot be used as reference sites without Customer consent. Additionally, the Supplier must promptly notify the Customer of any change to the country location or data centre where Customer Confidential Information is stored, hosted, or accessed.

clauses.description: The Recipient Party may disclose Confidential Information to its personnel and professional advisers under written confidentiality undertakings no less stringent than those in the contract. Upon the Customer’s request, the Supplier must complete a Third Party Security Assessment (onsite or remote, typically once per calendar year, but more frequently after a material cyber incident or regulatory direction) and provide full information to verify compliance. The Supplier indemnifies the Customer against losses arising from breach of confidentiality or regulatory obligations. The Customer or its auditor may conduct penetration testing on the Services and Supplier systems. The Supplier acknowledges strict banking secrecy and confidentiality laws, must ensure Customer Confidential Information remains accessible at all times (including during bankruptcy or business discontinuation), and must fully cooperate with threat-led penetration testing as required under DORA.

clauses.description: The Supplier must implement appropriate physical, technical, and organizational security measures (at least Good Industry Practice and the most stringent applicable standard) to protect Customer Confidential Information and Personal Data against accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, or unauthorized disclosure—ensuring confidentiality, integrity, availability, resilience, and timely restoration after incidents. These measures must be no less stringent than those used for the Supplier’s own data, and the Supplier must segregate Customer Personal Data from its own and other customers’ data. For devices processing Customer data, the Supplier must deploy mobile device management, containerization, remote wipe capabilities, and automatic updates/periodic scans to detect jailbreaking, rooting, tampering, or malware. The Supplier must also assist the other party in implementing appropriate security measures and, in joint controller scenarios, comply with the Information Security Controls at all times.

clauses.clause_key: Record Retention, Information and Audit Rights
clauses.description: The Supplier must maintain a complete audit trail and records for at least seven years after contract termination, and assist the Customer in retaining copies. The Customer, Customer Group, Auditors, and Regulatory Authorities have extensive audit and inspection rights (with seven days’ notice, or less in emergencies or as required by law) to verify charges, confidentiality, system integrity, performance, regulatory compliance, and to examine all resources, premises, systems, policies, and records (excluding Supplier cost/margin data). Audits may include penetration testing, threat and vulnerability assessments, running the Customer’s own software on Supplier systems, and extend to Subcontractors. The Supplier must conduct regular internal audits, train personnel, and provide all reasonable assistance—including making data and records available within seven days (or less), permitting onsite inspections, and allowing copies. The Supplier must also share any internal or external audit results relevant to the supplies within 30 days, and perform an annual internal audit of compliance. Each party bears its own audit costs unless a breach (non-trivial) is found, in which case the Supplier reimburses the Customer. Remediation of any non-compliance or risk is required promptly upon Customer request. These audit obligations survive contract termination. Additionally, the Supplier must respond to audit information requests within 24 hours.

clauses.clause_key: Exit Management & Data Return or Destruction
clauses.description: Upon the Customer’s request or when retention is no longer needed, the Supplier must delete, destroy, or return all Customer Confidential Information and storage media using an auditable process that prevents recreation or access—for electronic media, a sanitation process meeting Good Industry Practice (e.g., NIST SP800-88 or DoD 5220.22-M), and for documents, cross-shredding or secure waste disposal. The Supplier must certify in writing that deletion or destruction has occurred. On contract termination or expiry, the Supplier must immediately deliver all Customer property and Deliverables (including unencrypted electronic copies in a requested format) and irretrievably delete remaining copies. The Supplier must also ensure Customer Confidential Information remains accessible to the Customer at all times during the contract term, including in cases of bankruptcy or insolvency, and comply with the Exit Management Annexure.

clauses.clause_key: Exit Management & Data Return or Destruction @ IT Outsourced Managed Services Schedule
clauses.description: If a Step In Event occurs or is reasonably likely (including data breach, confidentiality breach, legal violation, regulatory direction, or reputational risk), the Customer may issue a Step In Notice and take control of the impacted managed services itself, appoint a third party, or second employees to the Supplier. During step-in, the Customer pays no charges for those services; if the step-in follows a Supplier breach, the Supplier must reimburse the Customer’s excess costs. The Supplier must fully cooperate, grant licenses, provide access to systems and locations, and take steps to recommence performance. If the Supplier cannot demonstrate readiness to resume within 90 days, the Customer may terminate. Regarding employee transfer, the parties do not intend any Supplier personnel or employment claims to transfer to the Customer; the Supplier warrants no such transfer, indemnifies the Customer against any claims, and the Customer may terminate any transferred rights. The benefit of this clause may be passed to a replacement supplier, and the Order Form may address transfer of Customer personnel to the Supplier.

clauses.clause_key: Exit Management & Data Return or Destruction @ Business Process Outsourcing Schedule
clauses.description: The parties do not intend any employee transfer regulations to transfer Supplier personnel or employment claims to the Customer, any Customer service provider, or a replacement supplier; the Supplier warrants no such transfer, indemnifies the Customer against any claims, and the Customer may immediately terminate any transferred rights—with the benefit of this clause transferable to a replacement supplier, while the Order Form may address transfer of Customer personnel to the Supplier. Separately, if a Step In Event occurs or is reasonably likely, the Customer may require the Supplier to take remedial steps at the Supplier’s cost (with good faith discussions on costs). If the Supplier fails to act, the Customer may take control of the impacted services, appoint a third party, or second employees to the Supplier. During step-in, the Customer pays no charges for those services, is responsible for performance, and the Supplier must reimburse any excess costs if the step-in follows a Supplier breach. The Supplier must fully cooperate, grant licences, and provide access to systems and locations. After the Supplier demonstrates readiness (subject to Customer approval), services recommence; if not ready within 90 days, the Customer may terminate for material breach. The Supplier is not liable for any degradation of services during the step-in period.

clauses.clause_key: Incident Management  (Notification of Breach and Changes)
clauses.description: The Supplier must promptly notify the Customer of any Material Cyber Security Incident without undue delay—within 1 hour for a Relevant Incident, or within 24 hours (2 hours for India Material Outsourcing) for all others—using specified communication channels (primary email and secondary phone) and providing mandatory details via the Customer’s reporting template. The Supplier must assist with investigations, perform a root cause analysis (RCA) within 10 days (final RCA within 28 days if ongoing analysis), implement corrective measures to prevent recurrence, and notify the Customer of any material changes to systems, data location, subcontractors, or applicable laws affecting compliance. The Customer may notify Regulatory Authorities without breaching confidentiality obligations.
